findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow the `_HAVE_ASTGREP` AC verification command to the two changed files: e.g. `git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP tests/unit/grounding/test_conformance.py tests/unit/grounding/test_coverage_reconstruction.py` returning no matches (test_engine_b.py legitimately retains _HAVE_ASTGREP in live skipif decorators and must not be touched).
evidence: tests/unit/grounding/test_engine_b.py:36: _HAVE_ASTGREP = engine_b.astgrep_binary() is not None
tests/unit/grounding/test_engine_b.py:231: @pytest.mark.skipif(not _HAVE_ASTGREP, reason='ast-grep not installed')
tests/unit/grounding/test_engine_b.py:360: @pytest.mark.skipif(not (_HAVE_SEMGREP and _HAVE_ASTGREP), reason='engines not installed')
Plan body correctly notes test_engine_b.py is not a removal target — but the AC's git grep command doesn't scope to the two changed files
findings.finding: The AC for _HAVE_ASTGREP removal states 'verified by `git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP` returning no matches', but `tests/unit/grounding/test_engine_b.py` (not a change target) defines AND uses `_HAVE_ASTGREP` in two live `@pytest.mark.skipif` decorators (lines 36, 231, 360). After the plan is executed correctly, `git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP` will still return three matches in `test_engine_b.py`, making the stated verification command un-satisfiable.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria: '[ ] _HAVE_ASTGREP removed from both files, verified by `git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP` returning no matches'
scenarios: Operator runs `git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP` post-change expecting no output; gets 3 hits from test_engine_b.py; AC cannot be attested as passing even though the removal was done correctly.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC line with: '`git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP tests/unit/grounding/test_conformance.py tests/unit/grounding/test_coverage_reconstruction.py` returns no matches (test_engine_b.py retains its live _HAVE_ASTGREP; the plan body already documents this correctly).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add run_differs.py (_read_local_ticket_full, line 159) as a co-target of the AI-1 fix, applying the same explicit-env construction (stripping REBAR_SYNC_PULL and REBAR_NO_SYNC on the live path).
evidence: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/run_differs.py:159: _env = dict(_os.environ, REBAR_SYNC_PULL='off') if no_sync else None
Plan's Scope section lists only 'reconcile_helpers.py (AI-1)'; run_differs.py is absent.
.rebar/cache/plan-review/62a3-a49d-04a0-4f18/85615ef12fa8c56eef96.json and 0bafeef03be5211dcb17.json (prior reviews corroborating this gap)
findings.finding: The AI-1 fix is described only for reconcile_helpers.py but the identical env-inheritance bug exists at run_differs.py line 159 (`_env = dict(_os.environ, REBAR_SYNC_PULL='off') if no_sync else None`). The plan names run_differs.py nowhere in scope or in the regression-test AC, leaving the same defect unremediated in that sibling module.
scenarios: The reconciler's differ phase calls _read_local_ticket_full via run_differs.py with no_sync=False; an ambient REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off in the caller's env is inherited unchanged and suppresses the live sync pull — the same bug the plan claims to fix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the _HAVE_ASTGREP removal scope: either (a) restrict the AC git-grep check to the two named files only (e.g., `git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP -- tests/unit/grounding/test_conformance.py tests/unit/grounding/test_coverage_reconstruction.py`), or (b) acknowledge test_engine_b.py's live uses and exclude that file from the 'no matches' claim.
evidence: Plan states: 'Remove the dead _HAVE_ASTGREP capability flag from test_conformance.py and test_coverage_reconstruction.py: it is never used in any skipif'
AC states: 'verified by git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP returning no matches'
test_engine_b.py:36: _HAVE_ASTGREP = engine_b.astgrep_binary() is not None
test_engine_b.py:231: @pytest.mark.skipif(not _HAVE_ASTGREP, reason='ast-grep not installed')
test_engine_b.py:360: @pytest.mark.skipif(not (_HAVE_SEMGREP and _HAVE_ASTGREP), reason='engines not installed')
Plan nowhere mentions test_engine_b.py in the _HAVE_ASTGREP removal scope
findings.finding: The plan states _HAVE_ASTGREP 'is never used in any skipif' and the AC verification command ('git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP returning no matches') presupposes removal from only test_conformance.py and test_coverage_reconstruction.py achieves a zero-match result. But test_engine_b.py defines _HAVE_ASTGREP at line 36 and uses it in live @pytest.mark.skipif decorators at lines 231 and 360. The plan omits test_engine_b.py from the removal scope, so the AC git-grep check will return matches and fail — the verification protocol is broken by the plan's own under-specified scope.
findings.location: Tier 1 — '_HAVE_ASTGREP removed' bullet; AC: '[ ] _HAVE_ASTGREP removed from both files, verified by git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP returning no matches'
scenarios: Executor runs `git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP` after applying the plan's two-file removal; grep returns 3 hits in test_engine_b.py; the AC is marked failing even though the two named files were correctly cleaned.

If executor interprets the AC literally and also removes _HAVE_ASTGREP from test_engine_b.py, the live skipif decorators at lines 231 and 360 break (NameError), causing test_engine_b.py to fail to import.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC to: '`git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP -- tests/unit/grounding/test_conformance.py tests/unit/grounding/test_coverage_reconstruction.py` returns no matches (test_engine_b.py retains its live _HAVE_ASTGREP skipif guards).' Also update the Scope prose to note that test_engine_b.py's _HAVE_ASTGREP IS used in skipif and is intentionally retained.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the Tier-2 symbol removal AC so `git grep -w <symbol>` is either (a) a PRE-removal deadness check with expected output 'exactly one match (the definition)', or (b) replaced with a POST-removal check `git grep -w <symbol>; [ $? -ne 0 ]` (exit 1 = no matches = symbol is gone).
evidence: Plan quote: 'git grep -w <symbol> returning only the removed definition line'
After removal, git grep searches the working tree and finds nothing — 'the removed definition line' no longer exists in the tree, so zero lines are returned, contradicting the stated expectation.
This is verify-command defect taxonomy #7 (missing prerequisite-state ordering): the grep makes sense as a PRE-removal deadness check but is written as a POST-removal completion check.
findings.finding: The Tier-2 symbol removal AC says `git grep -w <symbol>` should return 'only the removed definition line', but after removal the working-tree grep will return ZERO lines — making the stated expected output directly contradict the completion state. An executor following the AC post-removal will see no output and be unable to tell whether the criterion is satisfied or the grep failed.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — Tier-2 bullet: 'verified by `ruff check --select F821` clean on the changed files … plus `git grep -w <symbol>` returning only the removed definition line'
scenarios: Executor completes all removals, runs `git grep -w _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`, sees no output, and cannot determine whether the AC is met or the grep pattern is wrong.
A reviewer interpreting the AC literally may mark it failed because zero lines ≠ 'only the removed definition line'.
findings.suggested_fix:
Split into two explicit checks in the AC:
1. Pre-removal (deadness gate): `git grep -w <symbol>` returns exactly the definition line — confirms no live usages.
2. Post-removal (completion gate): `git grep -w <symbol>` returns no output (exit code 1) — confirms the symbol is gone from the tree.
Alternatively collapse to: 'after the change, `git grep -w <symbol>` returns no matches (exit 1) for each removed symbol, AND `ruff check --select F821` reports no undefined names on the changed files.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either extend the AI-1 fix and its regression test to also cover `run_differs._read_local_ticket_full` (line 159, identical `_env = dict(_os.environ, REBAR_SYNC_PULL='off') if no_sync else None` pattern), or explicitly document why the exclusion is intentional in the 'Retained' section.
evidence: Plan states: 'reconcile_helpers.py (AI-1): when no_sync=False, _env=None inherits the parent env' — names only reconcile_helpers.py
run_differs.py:159: `_env = dict(_os.environ, REBAR_SYNC_PULL='off') if no_sync else None` — identical pattern, same subprocess env-inheritance bug
run_differs.py is not listed in Scope, Tier 1, Tier 2, or Retained sections
ABSENCE: no justification for excluding run_differs.py from the fix
findings.finding: The plan's AI-1 fix is scoped exclusively to reconcile_helpers.py, but run_differs._read_local_ticket_full (run_differs.py:159) contains the IDENTICAL bug pattern: `_env = dict(_os.environ, REBAR_SYNC_PULL='off') if no_sync else None` — when no_sync=False, _env=None and a caller's ambient REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off silently suppresses the live sync pull. This co-located bug is neither a fix target nor listed in 'Retained' with a justification for exclusion, making the AI-1 scope a false exclusion that the tools can and do refute.
scenarios: A caller with REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off in its environment invokes run_differs._read_local_ticket_full with no_sync=False; the subprocess inherits the suppressed-sync env variable, silently skipping the live sync pull — identical defect to the AI-1 bug being fixed.
After the fix, tests covering reconcile_helpers pass but the identical bug in run_differs.py persists undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the same explicit-env construction to run_differs._read_local_ticket_full at line 159, mirror the regression test scenario for that code path, and add it to the Scope section under AI-1.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
verify command `git grep -n _HAVE_ASTGREP` greps the unanchored word '_HAVE_ASTGREP' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review__HAVE_ASTGREP'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"_HAVE_ASTGREP":')
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: AC4 ('Exactly the enumerated Tier-2 symbols removed; each verified unreferenced; retained symbols untouched') has no stated proving command. The umbrella AC6 ('make lint + mypy clean; the touched test modules pass in-session') does NOT prove symbol removal: mypy and lint will not report an error for a symbol that was already dead and is now simply absent — they pass whether or not the removals happened.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'Verified by: `python scripts/verify_symbol_removals.py` (or equivalent per-file grep assertions) exits 0 — each dead symbol returns 0 hits; each retained symbol returns ≥1 hit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AI-1 live-path env fix to also strip REBAR_NO_SYNC (the permanent alias for REBAR_SYNC_PULL per _config_sources.py:233) from the explicit env when no_sync=False, and add a corresponding regression-test assertion for an ambient REBAR_NO_SYNC=1.
evidence: src/rebar/_config_sources.py:233: "REBAR_NO_SYNC": ("sync", "pull", "REBAR_SYNC_PULL")
src/rebar/_deprecations.py:80: _permanent("env", "REBAR_NO_SYNC", "REBAR_SYNC_PULL")
Plan says 'strip the key on the live path' but names only REBAR_SYNC_PULL; REBAR_NO_SYNC is absent from the fix description and from the AC regression-test spec.
.rebar/cache/plan-review/62a3-a49d-04a0-4f18/fc701852eaa34bf411c7.json (prior review corroborating this gap)
findings.finding: The AI-1 env-construction fix strips REBAR_SYNC_PULL from the live-path env but does not also strip REBAR_NO_SYNC, its permanent alias. _config_sources.py line 233 shows the subprocess's config layer resolves REBAR_NO_SYNC=1 to sync.pull=off identically to REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off. After the fix, a caller running with ambient REBAR_NO_SYNC=1 still silently suppresses the live sync pull — the root cause (env inheritance) is only partially remediated.
findings.location: Scope / Tier 1 / reconcile_helpers.py (AI-1); AC line 'regression test asserts the live path strips an ambient REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off'
scenarios: A CI environment sets REBAR_NO_SYNC=1 globally; the reconcile live path inherits it; the explicit env built by the fix includes REBAR_NO_SYNC=1 (not stripped); the subprocess disables sync pull despite no_sync=False — the bug survives.
findings.suggested_fix: When building the explicit env for the live path (no_sync=False), pop both keys: `env = {k: v for k, v in os.environ.items() if k not in ('REBAR_SYNC_PULL', 'REBAR_NO_SYNC')}`. Update the AC regression-test spec to assert both `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` AND `REBAR_NO_SYNC=1` are stripped on the live path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (and AC) that the explicit env is built as a copy of the parent environment (`os.environ.copy()`) with REBAR_SYNC_PULL selectively stripped or added — and add an AC assertion that other env vars are preserved on the live path.
evidence: 'Build an explicit env; set REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off only when no_sync=True; strip the key on the live path'
AC: 'the live path strips an ambient REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off' — tests only REBAR_SYNC_PULL, silent about other vars
No mention of os.environ.copy() or equivalent inheritance strategy
findings.finding: The AI-1 fix says 'Build an explicit env' but never specifies whether the explicit env is a copy of the parent environment (minus REBAR_SYNC_PULL) or a fresh/empty mapping. If fresh, all other ambient env vars (PATH, HOME, credentials, proxy settings, etc.) are silently dropped for the subprocess — an unacknowledged behavioral break for callers that rely on env inheritance beyond REBAR_SYNC_PULL. The AC only asserts REBAR_SYNC_PULL stripping/setting; it does not require that other env vars are preserved.
findings.location: Scope > Tier 1 > reconcile_helpers.py (AI-1); Acceptance criteria item 1
scenarios: Implementation uses os.environ.copy() minus key — no break, but plan doesn't confirm this
Implementation uses {} or {} | {KEY: val} — PATH/credentials/proxy vars are dropped, subprocess behavior silently changes
findings.suggested_fix: In the AI-1 description add: 'The explicit env is constructed as `env = os.environ.copy()` followed by `env.pop("REBAR_SYNC_PULL", None)` on the live path or `env["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"` on the no_sync path.' Extend the regression test to assert a bystander env var (e.g. PATH) is present in the subprocess env on both paths.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out statement to the plan (e.g. 'All changes land in a single Gerrit CL; revert via `git revert <sha>` is the back-out path; the LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 gates are the primary safeguard').
evidence: ABSENCE: no 'rollback', 'revert', 'back-out', or 'expand-contract' language anywhere in the plan
AI-1 changes runtime subprocess environment behavior (not merely dead code removal)
Tier-2 removals are irreversible deletions of source symbols
findings.finding: No rollback or back-out plan is stated for any change in this ticket, including the behavioral change in reconcile_helpers.py (AI-1). T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing for destructive/behavior-changing steps — acknowledgement of the change alone is insufficient per the rubric.
scenarios: AI-1 regression discovered post-merge: no documented back-out procedure
A Tier-2 symbol turns out to be used by an out-of-repo consumer: deletion is already landed
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: 'All changes are contained in one CL. If a post-merge regression is found, `git revert <sha>` restores all symbols and the prior env-inheritance behavior atomically. No schema or data migrations are involved, making revert safe.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] (Advisory) Consider whether the behavioral bug fix (AI-1 + regression test) warrants its own Gerrit change separate from the pure dead-symbol removals, to reduce revert blast-radius and review cognitive load — the plan's single-change rationale (reduce churn) is legitimate, but the heterogeneous change kinds across unrelated subsystems are worth an explicit team acknowledgment.
evidence: 'This ticket remediates the reliability/maintainability subset in one change' — explicit batching rationale, but the subset itself spans bug fix, new test, type alignment, and dead-code removal.
Tier 1 items: env bug fix (behavioral change + regression test), dead flag removal, new category test, BatchRunner signature alignment — four distinct change kinds.
Tier 2: dead symbol removal across acli_graph, reconcile_check, reads, resolve, graph helpers, reducer tests, ticket_unblock tests, overlap_judge — genuinely diffuse across unrelated subsystems.

The AI-1 env fix is a correctness/reliability change; BatchRunner alignment is a type-safety change; Tier 2 is purely cosmetic dead-code hygiene — these have distinct reasons to change and distinct actors (reliability engineer vs. type-safety vs. cleanliness).
findings.finding: The plan bundles four heterogeneous change kinds — a behavioral bug fix with regression test (AI-1 env stripping), a test-gap addition (AI-3b), a type/signature alignment (BatchRunner), and a batch of pure dead-symbol removals (Tier 2) — across unrelated subsystems (reconcile helpers, runners, acli graph, resolve, grounding, and multiple test modules). Each outcome is independently valuable, testable, and releasable on its own, which is the G5 threshold for surfacing a decomposition concern.
findings.location: Scope section (Tier 1 + Tier 2) and Context paragraph
scenarios: If the AI-1 env fix introduces an unexpected regression, the entire change (including unrelated dead-code removals) must be reverted or the fix extracted post-hoc, increasing revert blast radius.
A reviewer focused on the behavioral AI-1 fix must also review dozens of unrelated symbol removals across unrelated test modules, increasing review latency and cognitive load.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/workflow/test_batch_step.py`'s `_GarbagePlanRunner.run` to the signature-alignment scope: update its override to `run(self, req, agent_runner=None)` to match the updated base contract and keep mypy clean.
evidence: tests/unit/workflow/test_batch_step.py:193: class _GarbagePlanRunner(_ex.BatchRunner):
tests/unit/workflow/test_batch_step.py:197:     def run(self, req, agent_runner):
Plan names only BatchRunner, DefaultBatchRunner, CodeReviewBatchRunner as targets; test_batch_step.py is not listed
AC requires 'make lint + mypy clean'
findings.finding: The plan makes `agent_runner` optional (default `None`) in `BatchRunner`, `DefaultBatchRunner`, and `CodeReviewBatchRunner`, but does not enumerate `tests/unit/workflow/test_batch_step.py`'s `_GarbagePlanRunner` as a consumer requiring update. `_GarbagePlanRunner` subclasses `BatchRunner` and overrides `run(self, req, agent_runner)` WITHOUT defaulting `agent_runner`. Once the base gains `agent_runner=None`, mypy may flag this override as a Liskov-violation (override signature is more restrictive than the base), which would break the 'mypy clean' AC.
scenarios: After the plan is applied, mypy runs on test_batch_step.py and flags _GarbagePlanRunner.run for having a non-default agent_runner parameter when the base now declares agent_runner=None (mypy --strict would emit an error on the override signature mismatch).
findings.suggested_fix: In test_batch_step.py line 197, change `def run(self, req, agent_runner):` to `def run(self, req, agent_runner=None):`.

findings.finding: The AI-3b scope specifies adding a test to 'test_validation_depth.py' (a file that does not exist in the repo) proving 'input-contract validation is category-agnostic', but provides no processing protocol: it names neither the validator function under test, the inputs to supply, nor the assertion to make. An executor cannot implement this test without inferring the entire test body from a single-sentence description.
findings.location: Scope section: 'test_validation_depth.py (AI-3b)' and AC line '[ ] AI-3b non-review category test added and passing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a behavioral AC (e.g., a 1-arg call test) for DefaultBatchRunner.run and CodeReviewBatchRunner.run verifying they execute without error when agent_runner is omitted, mirroring the ProductionBatchRunner 1-arg call already cited in the plan.
evidence: AC states only 'mypy clean' for the signature alignment — no behavioral/runtime test for DefaultBatchRunner or CodeReviewBatchRunner with agent_runner omitted
Plan notes ProductionBatchRunner 'tested via a 1-arg call' but does not assert equivalent coverage exists for DefaultBatchRunner or CodeReviewBatchRunner
Changed/defaulted behavior (agent_runner now optional in these classes) gets only type-level verification, not runtime behavioral verification
findings.finding: The BatchRunner.run signature change (making agent_runner optional in DefaultBatchRunner and CodeReviewBatchRunner) has no specified behavioral test — only mypy validation is required by the AC. If the implementation of these runners uses agent_runner internally without None-guarding, mypy will not catch a runtime crash when called with one argument, and the suite could go green while the newly-defaulted path is broken.
findings.location: Tier 1 — runners.py + code_review/batch_runner.py (#450); AC: 'BatchRunner.run signatures aligned; mypy clean'
scenarios: DefaultBatchRunner.run(batch) called without agent_runner crashes at runtime because implementation accesses agent_runner without None guard — mypy passes, suite goes green, but production call fails
CodeReviewBatchRunner similarly broken for the newly-optional argument path
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: 'A unit/integration test calls DefaultBatchRunner.run(batch) and CodeReviewBatchRunner.run(batch) without agent_runner and asserts no TypeError/AttributeError is raised, analogous to the existing ProductionBatchRunner 1-arg call test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the runtime behavior when agent_runner=None is passed to DefaultBatchRunner.run: either (a) raise ValueError early with a clear message, (b) fall back to FakeAgentRunner(), or (c) explicitly document that DefaultBatchRunner always requires a non-None agent_runner and the optional default is purely for signature compatibility (with a runtime assert).
evidence: Plan: 'make agent_runner optional in the base BatchRunner, DefaultBatchRunner, and CodeReviewBatchRunner too'
runners.py:113: DefaultBatchRunner.run calls self._run_one(...) which calls agent_runner.run(ctx) at line 153 — no None guard
runners.py:93-95: base BatchRunner.run signature takes agent_runner: AgentStepRunner (no default)
Plan AC specifies only 'mypy clean', not a runtime guard or fallback for None agent_runner in DefaultBatchRunner
findings.finding: The plan makes agent_runner optional (= None default) in BatchRunner.run, DefaultBatchRunner.run, and CodeReviewBatchRunner.run for mypy consistency, but specifies no fallback or runtime guard for the None case. DefaultBatchRunner._run_one calls agent_runner.run(ctx) unconditionally — passing None causes an AttributeError with no specified handling. The plan cites only 'mypy clean' as validation, leaving the None-agent_runner runtime path undefined.
findings.location: Tier 1 — 'runners.py + code_review/batch_runner.py (#450)' bullet; AC: '[ ] BatchRunner.run signatures aligned; mypy clean'
scenarios: A caller constructs DefaultBatchRunner() and calls .run(req) (omitting agent_runner), relying on the new optional default. DefaultBatchRunner._run_one calls agent_runner.run(ctx), raising AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'run' — no fallback is specified.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
